Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered throughout the course. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings, maybe four or five hours on most days, but it rarely went according to plan. I found that reading and writing out notes worked best for me, so I'd print materials or copy things into my own documents. Some weeks I was really on top of things, other weeks life just got in the way and I had to catch up in bursts.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd love to say I had a consistent schedule, but the reality is that I'd often ramp up my studying when assignments were approaching. When things were calmer, I'd still study but maybe not as intensely. Having twelve online courses meant I was constantly juggling priorities, so regularity was more of an ideal than a reality for me.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there were new announcements or seeing what assignments were coming up. Sometimes I'd log in because a classmate mentioned something in our group chat that I'd missed. Honestly, the fear of falling behind was a big motivator—I'd get a little anxious wondering if I'd missed a deadline, so I'd check just to calm my nerves.
Q4: There were a few weeks where logging in became more automatic, especially when I was actively working on a module. I'd wake up, grab my coffee, and just open the LMS as part of my morning routine. But that habit would fade once I finished that particular section or when other courses demanded more attention. It never quite became a permanent habit for me.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were my lifeline—I learn best by reading and then rewriting concepts in my own words. The discussion boards were also helpful when I actually participated because seeing other students' perspectives clarified things I was confused about. I also appreciated when instructors posted detailed feedback on assignments because it gave me something concrete to work with.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't watch many of the videos all the way through, even though I know they were supposed to be helpful. I'd skim them or watch at 2x speed just to get the gist. They just didn't fit my learning style as well as written content did. Some of the optional readings I also skipped when time got tight—I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to the assignments or exams. If I knew understanding a concept would help me write a better paper or answer exam questions, I was all in. I also valued activities where I felt like I was actually learning something new rather than just going through the motions to tick a box.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With required work, there's that external pressure pushing you forward, but optional stuff? I'd tell myself I'd get to it later and then never did. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because a peer recommended it or I was genuinely curious—not because I felt obligated.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them on my tablet without being connected. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd rewrite key concepts and create my own summaries. My study group would share notes through WhatsApp too, so a lot of collaboration happened completely off-platform.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours going through printed materials or working on draft essays in Word documents. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that—it would just show that I hadn't logged in. I'd also re-read my own notes from previous sessions, which was a big part of how I prepared for exams but left no digital trace.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty much my standard approach. I'd download everything I needed in one session, then work through it over the next few days without logging back in. It was more efficient for me because I could study without distractions and didn't need to worry about internet connectivity. The LMS would show one access, but I might have spent ten hours with that material.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really tough. I was juggling multiple courses and my attendance dropped because I was prioritizing assignments over live sessions. There was a period where I felt moderately anxious about keeping up, and I'd just freeze sometimes instead of tackling things head-on. My peer support group really helped pull me through those moments.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities—like family stuff and my extracurriculars—meant I sometimes had to make hard choices about what to engage with. Fatigue especially affected my ability to participate in discussions; I'd read posts but not have the energy to respond thoughtfully.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing in the background while my mind was completely elsewhere. I was technically 'engaging' with the content, but nothing was sticking. It felt like I was just going through the motions to feel productive without actually being productive.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on some assignments, especially the ones that felt overwhelming or unclear. When I didn't fully understand what was expected, I'd put it off hoping clarity would come later. Sometimes I avoided discussion posts because I felt like I didn't have anything smart to add. Looking back, starting earlier would have helped my completion rate.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become incredibly focused and productive. The LMS would probably show spikes in my activity right before due dates. Without deadlines, I'd drift—so the structure they provided was actually really important for keeping me on track, even if it meant last-minute stress.
Q17: Feedback had a big impact on my motivation. When I got constructive comments, I felt energized to do better on the next assignment. But when feedback was delayed or minimal, I'd lose some momentum because I wasn't sure if I was on the right track. The peer support I received also functioned like informal feedback and kept me going during tough stretches.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag for me. Having clear modules helped me know what to focus on, but sometimes the pacing felt off with my other commitments. When everything was well-organized with clear expectations, I engaged more. When things felt scattered or when multiple deadlines collided, my participation dropped because I couldn't keep up.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier because I could study in my own way. The discussion forums were also nice when they were active. What made it harder was when important information was buried in videos I had to watch entirely to find one key point. Also, when navigation was confusing, I'd waste time just trying to find what I needed.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce the actual work. Maybe they're watching videos and reading everything but having trouble translating that into assignments. It could also be anxiety—being present and trying but feeling paralyzed when it comes to submitting something for evaluation.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS activity wasn't super high, but I still managed a Merit grade because so much of my real learning happened offline. I'd download materials, study from my notes, and work through problems on paper. The platform only captured a fraction of the effort I put in, so low activity didn't mean low engagement for me.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how often they logged in. They can track submission times and discussion participation. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just clicking through, and the mental and emotional effort someone puts in. They're like seeing footprints without knowing the journey.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to get a grade. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts, and feeling like the learning matters. For me, engagement also includes the emotional investment—feeling motivated to push through even when things get hard, which my peer support group really helped with.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on my study group and peer support to stay motivated. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes at my desk or the mental effort of working through concepts in my head during my commute. It also wouldn't show that despite moderate anxiety, I found ways to keep going—that resilience isn't visible in login data.
